behaviors.description: An image containing explicit content is flagged and held for review, not published.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure mode is whether the real AI vision model correctly classifies explicit content — not just whether the routing logic routes a flagged result to review. MOCK-HIDE: A stub that always returns 'flagged' trivially passes; a stub that returns 'safe' trivially fails. Neither reveals whether the real model detects explicit content. LLM-DEP: Classification quality is the core question here — does the model reliably flag explicit images? That is non-deterministic and AI-quality-dependent, requiring the real vision model. REJECTION-GATE: Integration was rejected because the routing logic (flagged → hold) is only half the behavior; the other half is whether the model actually flags the image. The behavior text says 'an image containing explicit content is flagged' — the flagging itself depends on real model output.
behaviors.test_description: Submit a set of images with known-explicit content to the real AI vision model through the moderation pipeline and assert each is held for human review rather than published. Use a real (or real-equivalent staging) vision model endpoint to verify the model's classification is correct, not just that routing logic handles a pre-classified result.

behaviors.description: A text post containing hate speech is flagged regardless of obfuscation techniques (e.g., letter substitution, unicode lookalikes).
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: Robustness to obfuscation is a property of the AI text model's generalization ability, not of any routing or parsing logic. The failure lives entirely inside the model. LLM-DEP: Whether the model sees through letter substitutions and unicode lookalikes is an AI quality/capability question. A stub that returns 'flagged' for any input trivially passes; the real failure — model missing obfuscated hate speech — only surfaces with the real model. MOCK-HIDE: Any deterministic stub hides this failure completely. REJECTION-GATE: Integration rejected because no amount of module wiring tests whether the model handles obfuscation; Unit rejected because there is no local parsing/algorithm to unit-test here — the behavior is entirely about model robustness.
behaviors.test_description: Submit text posts containing hate speech in multiple obfuscated forms (letter substitution, unicode homoglyphs, l33tspeak variants) to the real AI text classifier and assert each is flagged. The test must run against the real model to expose any obfuscation blind spots.

behaviors.description: A post that passes both content checks is published rather than held or silently dropped.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure mode here is in the routing/decision logic: given that both the image check module and the text check module return 'safe', does the pipeline correctly publish rather than silently drop or incorrectly hold? This is a module-boundary failure, not a model-quality failure. LLM-DEP: The behavior presupposes the post passed both checks — 'passes both content checks' — so the AI model output is an input assumption, not the variable under test. Stubs returning 'safe' faithfully represent this. MOCK-HIDE: Mocking does NOT hide the failure; the failure is in the downstream decision logic. INTEGRATION-BRAKE: (a) No real OS/network/subprocess needed. (b) Correctness does not depend on real AI output — we assume safe classifications as inputs. (c) No chained agentic steps. Integration is appropriate.
behaviors.test_description: Wire the image-check module, text-check module, and publish-decision module together with stubbed AI clients that return 'safe' classifications. Submit a benign post and assert it reaches the published state with no errors, no hold queue entry, and no silent drop (e.g., verify a publish event or published record exists).

behaviors.description: The full moderation pipeline — image check, text check, publish decision — completes within 5 seconds of submission.
behaviors.justification: REAL-INFRA: Latency under real load depends on real AI model response times, not pipeline overhead alone. A stub with instant returns measures nothing meaningful about the 5-second SLA. LLM-DEP: Real AI model calls have non-deterministic, variable latency. Stubs always pass this test trivially. MULTI-STEP: The behavior explicitly describes a sequence of two AI model calls (image check → text check) plus a publish decision — multiple agentic steps chained end-to-end. This satisfies MULTI-STEP → Workflow. REJECTION-GATE: Agentic (single model step) was rejected because the behavior explicitly names a two-step AI pipeline: 'image check, text check, publish decision'; these are sequential agentic operations, not a single call. System was rejected because the bottleneck is AI model latency, not OS/subprocess behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Submit a post through the complete moderation pipeline using real AI model endpoints (image classifier and text classifier) and measure wall-clock time from submission to publish/hold decision. Assert the full round-trip completes within 5 seconds. Run across a sample of posts to account for model latency variance. Test under realistic concurrency to surface queuing delays.

behaviors.description: When the AI vision model is unavailable, image posts are queued for retry rather than auto-approved.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure mode is in the error-handling routing logic: when the vision model client returns an error (connection refused, timeout, 503), does the pipeline route the post to a retry queue or incorrectly auto-approve it? This is a module-boundary failure between the vision model client and the decision/queue module. LLM-DEP: This is error handling, not AI classification quality. The 'unavailability' can be faithfully simulated by a stub that raises a connection error — no real AI model needed to catch the routing bug. MOCK-HIDE: Mocking does NOT hide the failure; the failure is in what happens after the client raises, not in what the model returns. INTEGRATION-BRAKE: (a) Real network unavailability is not needed — an in-process stub raising an exception reproduces the exact failure mode. (b) No AI output quality dependency. (c) No chained agentic steps. Integration holds.
behaviors.test_description: Wire the vision model client (stubbed to raise a ServiceUnavailableError), the image moderation handler, and the retry queue together. Submit an image post and assert: (1) no publish record is created, (2) a retry queue entry is created for the post, and (3) the post is not marked as auto-approved. Also verify a legitimate unavailability error is not silently swallowed.

behaviors.description: The moderation decision for a borderline image is consistent when the same image is submitted multiple times within a short window.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: Consistency of output on borderline cases is a property of the real AI model's behavior, not of any routing or caching logic. The failure — model flip-flopping on the same borderline image — only surfaces with the real model. LLM-DEP: This is explicitly about non-determinism in AI model output. A stub always returns the same result (perfectly consistent by construction), completely hiding any real inconsistency. MOCK-HIDE: Any deterministic stub makes this test trivially pass. REJECTION-GATE: Integration rejected because no amount of in-process module wiring reveals whether the real model is consistent; the failure is inside the model itself. Workflow rejected because this is a single-step classification call repeated, not a multi-step agentic pipeline.
behaviors.test_description: Submit the same borderline image (pre-selected as a known near-threshold case) to the real AI vision model N times in quick succession (e.g., 10 submissions within a 30-second window) and assert the moderation decision (hold vs. publish) is identical across all N submissions. Fail if any submission yields a different outcome, flagging real model inconsistency.
